title: linux find regex the golden goose of find
summary: A practical note on using find with regex so you can match several archive types without turning one shell command into a wall of conditions.
markdown_body:
# linux find regex the golden goose of find

When `find` turns into a long pile of `-name` clauses, it usually means the command is fighting the problem instead of describing it. This article leans into `-regex` and `-iregex` so a Linux admin can search for families of files in one readable line.

## Why it matters

- cleaner shell history
- easier copy and paste into runbooks
- less chance of missing one extension in a hurry

## Example

```bash
find ./ -regextype egrep -iregex ".*\.(gz|zip|tar|rar|7z)"
```

That pattern is easier to teach, easier to review, and easier to extend than a chain of `-name` flags.

title: Bash's corner expansion
summary: A short Linux productivity post on brace expansion and related shell shortcuts that save time when creating paths and repeating common patterns.
markdown_body:
# Bash's corner expansion

Brace expansion is one of those Linux shell features that stays invisible until you need it three times in a row. Then it becomes the obvious way to create directories, fan out file paths, or sketch a quick structure for a script test.

## Useful examples

```bash
mkdir -p project/{bin,etc,logs,tmp}
cp /etc/{hosts,resolv.conf} ./backup/
```

The point is not cleverness. The point is reducing repeated typing without making the command unreadable to the next person.

title: A few notes around some less documented Dynatrace Managed requirements
summary: Linux and platform notes around Dynatrace Managed prerequisites, especially where SELinux and environment assumptions can surprise an operator.
markdown_body:
# Dynatrace Managed notes that matter in real environments

Some deployment guides tell the happy path and stop there. Real systems usually have SELinux policies, network controls, and package-level assumptions that do not show up until late in a rollout.

## What to verify early

- SELinux mode and related policy expectations
- required open ports
- service account and file path assumptions
- whether an install guide quietly assumes a more permissive lab than your production estate

This kind of article exists to save the second install, not the first one.
